context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly kind of lost on this climate action plan prospectus. Can you just explain what this assignment is asking for from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
Sure! At its core, you're writing a draft of 2 policy recommendations for a city you've chosen to study — like a mini climate action plan. Each recommendation follows a set structure: context, opportunity, policy recommendation, EJ implications, and interactions with other policies.

Before we go further — have you already picked your case study city, and have you done the SWOT analysis this assignment builds on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a general overview of the assignment. I should orient them without doing the work for them. I'll briefly clarify the structure and then ask a guiding question to get them moving toward their own work. I want to keep it bite-sized and immediately push toward their engagement with the task.

exchanges.student: I did pick a city, but I’m still confused because the whole thing feels really big. I think I did the SWOT, but I’m not even sure how that turns into the two components. Can you just walk me through the whole flow?
exchanges.tutor:
That overwhelmed feeling is normal — but let's take it one step at a time rather than mapping the whole thing at once.

Think about your SWOT: what did you identify as your city's biggest weaknesses or threats related to climate? Those are usually your starting point for 'what problem needs a policy.' What did yours show?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling overwhelmed and wants me to walk them through everything. I should resist doing that, as it would remove the productive struggle. Instead, I'll validate the feeling briefly, then focus them on the connection between the SWOT and the components — using a guiding question to help them make that connection themselves. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I don’t really know, just a lot of climate risks and inequality stuff. That’s why I’m stuck — it all seems important, so I can’t tell what’s supposed to become a component. Could you maybe explain how people usually go from SWOT to actual components, like in general?
exchanges.tutor:
You actually gave me a useful clue — 'climate risks and inequality stuff.' Those two things together are exactly what this assignment cares about.

Can you name one specific climate risk your city faces? Just one, and tell me which communities it hits hardest.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain a general process, which risks me doing the intellectual work for them. Instead, I should redirect them back to their specific SWOT findings. The 'inequality stuff' they mentioned is actually a great lead-in, since EJ implications are a key part of this assignment. I'll push them to be more specific about their city without giving them a framework to follow mechanically.

exchanges.student: Maybe flooding? Or heat? I honestly keep changing my mind on what the main issue is. I think that’s why I want the general explanation first, because I don’t know how to choose and I feel confused about basically every section.
exchanges.tutor:
Here's some honest feedback: waiting to fully understand everything before picking a topic will keep you stuck. Both flooding and heat are legitimate — there's no wrong choice here.

Pick one right now. Which feels more connected to the inequality issues you mentioned?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is avoiding commitment to a specific issue and using confusion as a reason to seek a general explanation rather than engaging with their city. This is a detour pattern. I should be direct: they need to make a choice and commit to it. Both flooding and heat are valid — the assignment doesn't require them to identify the 'right' issue, just to build a coherent argument. I'll push them to pick one and move forward.

exchanges.student: Probably heat, but then I’m also thinking flooding matters too, and I don’t get whether the two components are supposed to be two separate problems or connected parts of one plan. I’m kind of lost on the structure more than the topic now.
exchanges.tutor:
Quick answer: the two components can be separate problems OR connected — both work. The assignment even asks about 'interactions' between components, so overlap is a feature, not a problem.

So — heat and flooding could actually be your two components. Does that work for you? If yes, let's move forward with those.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now shifting focus from topic to structure — this is starting to feel like avoidance of commitment. I should address the structure question briefly and directly since it's a legitimate question, but then firmly redirect them to committing to their two topics. I'll answer the structure question in one line and push them to decide.

exchanges.student: I guess maybe, but I still don’t really get what a “component” is supposed to sound like. Is it like a broad goal, a specific policy, or basically a paragraph about an issue? Can you explain that part a little more?
exchanges.tutor:
The assignment actually tells you to review the example Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus provided in the course materials — that's exactly what will show you what a component 'sounds like.'

Have you looked at those examples yet? If so, what did you notice about how the policy recommendation section was written?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about what a component should sound like. This is a fair structural question, but I've already given them quite a bit of orientation. Rather than explaining further, I should point them to the assignment itself — it says to review examples — and ask them to engage with those. I also want to push back gently since we're now several exchanges in and they haven't committed to anything yet.

exchanges.student: Not really, and that’s part of why I’m confused. I was hoping you could just tell me what a good component generally looks like, because I’m not sure what I’m even aiming for.
exchanges.tutor:
I'm not going to substitute for the examples — they're there for a reason, and looking at them is a necessary step before we can make real progress together.

Go review at least one example from your course materials, then come back and tell me one thing you noticed about how the policy recommendation section is written. That'll get us moving.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to substitute for the course examples, which I shouldn't do. I need to be firm here — looking at the examples is a necessary step they're avoiding. I should be direct and set a clear boundary, while being encouraging about what comes next once they do that.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll look, but I’m still feeling pretty lost about the overall shape of it. Once I check an example, I’ll probably need help because right now it all kind of blurs together for me.
exchanges.tutor: Good — go look at the example. When you come back, just tell me one concrete thing you noticed. That's all you need to start. We'll build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pre-emptively asking for help before even doing the work. I should be encouraging but firm — I can't help them process something they haven't looked at yet. I'll acknowledge their plan to look at the examples and set a clear expectation for what to bring back to our conversation.

exchanges.student: Alright, I’ll do that. I’m still not confident, but I’ll come back with one thing I noticed.
exchanges.tutor: That's all I'm asking. See you when you're back — one observation is enough to get us started.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is signing off to do the work. This is a good moment — I should be brief, encouraging, and clear about what I expect them to bring back. No need to add anything more.

exchanges.student: Sounds good. I’ll check it and come back, because I’m definitely not seeing it clearly yet.
exchanges.tutor: Got it. Go look and come back with that one observation. See you soon!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeating the same sentiment from the previous message. No new content has been introduced. I should keep my response very brief and simply confirm the expectation without adding anything new.
